exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.title: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.passage: One of (1) ...... most popular shopping websites today is Amazon. People use it to buy things that belong (2) ...... different sellers all over the world. Customers can find (3) ...... amazing variety of products, and (4) ...... website usually offers fast delivery. You can pay (5) ...... your order (6) ...... credit card or online payment. It's (7) ...... very convenient way to shop, especially if you only have a (8) ...... little time. Most products arrive (9) ...... excellent condition, and millions of users leave positive reviews every day. There are also a (10) ...... of special offers and discounts available online.
explanations: Fixed phrase → **a lot** of special offers.

exercises.title: B: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: After thinking for hours, she finally made her ...... . (DECIDE)
This smartphone is too ...... for me at the moment. (EXPENSE)
My old printer is completely ...... now. (USE)
Rich families in the past often employed ...... in large houses. (SERVE)
This shop sells stylish but ...... clothes for teenagers. (AFFORD)
The information in the advertisement was completely ...... . (TRUE)
Be careful with that necklace — it's very ...... . (VALUE)
If you ...... prices online, you can often save money. (COMPARE)

exercises.title: C: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
Could you lend me €20 until Monday? **from**
Could I ...... you until Monday?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is lend, so the correct answer is "borrow €20 from".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "borrow €20 from", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "borrow €20 from" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
I can't decide whether I prefer the black jacket or the grey one. **choose**
I can't ...... the black jacket and the grey one.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is decide between two, so the correct answer is "choose between".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "choose between", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "choose between" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
Why don't you return the trainers to the shop? **back**
Why don't you ...... to the shop?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is return trainers, so the correct answer is "take them back".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "take them back", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Owing money to several banks can be stressful. **debt**
It can be stressful when you ...... to several banks.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is owing money, so the correct answer is "are in debt".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
We must go faster or we'll miss the sale. **up**
We need to ...... or we'll miss the sale.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is go faster, so the correct answer is "hurry up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "hurry up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "hurry up" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
I paid for the headphones using notes and coins. **cash**
I paid for the headphones ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is using notes and coins, so the correct answer is "in cash".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in cash", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: E: Complete the sentences
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Let's ...... a look at the prices before buying anything.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "have".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Can I try these shoes ...... before I buy them?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Try shoes on before buying.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Those trainers are ...... sale this weekend.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: On sale = at a reduced price.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I can't ...... up my mind about which tablet to choose.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Make up my mind = decide.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The jeans are too ...... — I need a larger size.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Too small — need a larger size.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: That coat was a great ...... because it was 70% cheaper.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A great bargain = cheap price.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: She paid ...... cash because the card machine was broken.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Pay in cash.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I'd like to ...... a complaint about this broken charger.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Make a complaint.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The shop gave me a full ...... after I returned the phone.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Full refund after returning the phone.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: They were completely ...... of stock, so I bought it online.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Out of stock = none left in the shop.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: My brother is saving ...... for a new motorbike.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Saving up for something.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: He spent a ...... of money on computer games last month.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A lot of money = a large amount.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We bought this table at a real ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: At a real bargain = very cheap.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: You should always ...... prices before making a big purchase.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Always compare prices before buying.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
